issues.title: Tailscale Proxy Header Spoofing for Authentication Bypass
issues.description: The gateway's Tailscale passthrough authentication in src/gateway/auth.ts can be bypassed by any local process. The isTailscaleProxyRequest function (line 84-87) grants full Tailscale-authenticated access when: (1) the TCP connection originates from a loopback address (127.x.x.x or ::1), AND (2) the headers 'x-forwarded-for', 'x-forwarded-proto', and 'x-forwarded-host' are all present, AND (3) the 'tailscale-user-login' header is set. Any process running on the gateway host can open a TCP connection to 127.0.0.1:18789 and craft these headers without possessing any gateway token or password. The isLocalDirectRequest check (lines 45-58) correctly rejects purely-local requests that include forwarded headers, but this only applies to the 'local direct' path; the Tailscale path (lines 129-148) is evaluated first and accepts the spoofed headers.
issues.recommendation: Verify Tailscale proxy requests through a mechanism that cannot be spoofed by local processes. Options include: (1) Listening on the Tailscale-assigned IPv4 address only (100.64.0.0/10) instead of loopback when in Tailscale serve mode; (2) Using a mutual-TLS or Tailscale-specific socket path that only the Tailscale daemon can connect to; (3) Requiring a shared secret between the Tailscale proxy and the gateway; (4) Binding to a UNIX socket with filesystem permissions restricting access. At minimum, add a configuration warning that Tailscale mode is unsafe when 'bind=loopback' is set with 'allowTailscale=true'.
issues.evidence: The test in src/gateway/auth.test.ts line 96-116 confirms the exact exploit: connecting from 127.0.0.1 with x-forwarded-for, x-forwarded-proto, x-forwarded-host, and tailscale-user-login headers passes authentication as a Tailscale user without any token. The hasTailscaleProxyHeaders function (lines 75-82) only checks header presence, not origin authenticity. isTailscaleProxyRequest (lines 84-87) uses socket.remoteAddress — attacker-controlled from a loopback TCP connection.

issues.title: Timing Side-Channel in Gateway Token Authentication (Non-constant-time Comparison)
issues.description: In src/gateway/auth.ts, the 'token' authentication mode compares the provided token against the configured token using JavaScript's strict equality operator ('!==') at line 162, which is NOT timing-safe. This allows a remote attacker to measure response latency differences and perform a timing attack to recover the token character by character. The password mode correctly uses the timingSafeEqual wrapper (safeEqual function at lines 31-34), but the token mode does not use it. This inconsistency means token-mode gateways are vulnerable to timing oracle attacks that password-mode gateways are not.
issues.recommendation: Replace the direct string comparison on line 162 with the existing safeEqual function: change 'if (connectAuth.token !== auth.token)' to 'if (!safeEqual(connectAuth.token, auth.token))'. The safeEqual helper already exists in the same file and is correctly used for password comparison.
issues.evidence: safeEqual using crypto.timingSafeEqual is defined at lines 31-34 and called for password comparison at line 176. The token comparison at line 162 uses the '!==' operator which short-circuits on the first differing byte, leaking timing information. Over the WebSocket handshake over a local or Tailscale network, an attacker who can make many requests can measure response time differences to determine token length and content incrementally.

issues.title: Arbitrary Code Execution via Dynamic Import of Hook Transform Modules
issues.description: The hooks-mapping system in src/gateway/hooks-mapping.ts loads arbitrary JavaScript/TypeScript module files specified in the gateway configuration (hooks.mappings[].transform.module). At line 284, the code dynamically imports modules using await import(url) where the path is resolved from the config file. While this requires write access to the gateway config file (~/.clawdbot/config.json5), the transform module path is not sandboxed: it can point to any absolute file path on the system (line 299: if (path.isAbsolute(target)) return target). The loaded module is executed with full Node.js privileges in the gateway process. Any attacker who can write the config file or who can place a file at a path referenced in the config can achieve arbitrary code execution inside the gateway process.
issues.recommendation: Apply path sandboxing to restrict module paths to the transforms directory: reject absolute paths that escape the configured transformsDir, and verify the resolved path starts with the allowed base directory. Consider maintaining a registry of allowlisted transforms rather than loading arbitrary file paths. At minimum, log and warn loudly when a transform with an absolute path outside the config directory is loaded.
issues.evidence: Line 299: 'if (path.isAbsolute(target)) return target' — absolute paths are passed through without restriction. Line 284: 'const mod = (await import(url)) as Record<string, unknown>' — the file is dynamically imported and executed. An operator can configure 'transform.module: /tmp/malicious.js' and any incoming webhook that matches the mapping will execute that file with full gateway process privileges.

issues.title: Hooks Token Transmitted in URL Query Parameter (Cleartext Secret in Logs)
issues.description: The hooks token authentication in src/gateway/hooks.ts (lines 44-58) accepts the secret token via three channels: Authorization header (Bearer), custom X-Clawdbot-Token header, and a 'token' URL query parameter. When the token is passed as a query parameter, it is included in the full request URL which is typically written to access logs, reverse-proxy logs, server-side request logs, and browser history. This causes the secret hook token to leak in plaintext to anyone with access to any log files.
issues.recommendation: Remove support for the 'token' URL query parameter in extractHookToken. Tokens should only be accepted via headers (Authorization: Bearer or X-Clawdbot-Token) which are not included in server access logs. If query parameter support must be retained for compatibility, document the security risk clearly and consider deprecating it.
issues.evidence: Line 56 in src/gateway/hooks.ts: 'const queryToken = url.searchParams.get("token")' — the full URL including query string is visible in any HTTP access log, proxy log, or Nginx/Caddy access log. If the hooks endpoint is configured behind a reverse proxy (a common deployment), the proxy will log the full URL including the token.

issues.title: Uncontrolled Resource Consumption — No Rate Limiting on Hook Endpoints or WebSocket Connections
issues.description: The HTTP hook endpoints (/hooks/wake, /hooks/agent, and mapped paths) and the WebSocket gateway server have no rate-limiting applied. The hooks endpoints, when authenticated, trigger agent execution (full LLM inference) for every POST request. The /hooks/agent endpoint spawns an agent run with no debounce or concurrency limit per token. The WebSocket gateway server accepts an unlimited number of connections; each connection that passes the handshake registers presence and can immediately submit agent.send or chat.send requests. A valid token holder (or an attacker who has compromised a token) can flood the gateway with agent requests, exhausting system resources, API quota, and response budgets.
issues.recommendation: Implement per-token rate limiting for hook endpoints (e.g., max N agent dispatches per minute per token). For WebSocket connections, add a maximum concurrent-connection limit. Consider adding a configurable 'hooks.rateLimit' option in the gateway config that sets per-endpoint limits. The existing dedupe mechanism (src/gateway/server-http.ts uses idempotencyKey checks) partially mitigates re-dispatch for the same request, but does not limit distinct requests.
issues.evidence: src/gateway/server-http.ts lines 116-124: dispatchAgentHook is called directly after token validation with no rate limit check. The /hooks/agent endpoint returns HTTP 202 immediately and fires an async agent run. An attacker with a valid token can submit thousands of distinct agent requests in rapid succession, each spawning a separate LLM inference job.
